enrichment.short_description: AdminMart sells ready-made admin dashboard templates and UI kits for React, Angular, Vue, Next.js, Nuxt, and Bootstrap, helping developers ship back-office and SaaS interfaces faster.
enrichment.starting_price: Free templates available; premium templates start around 49 dollars as a one-time purchase
enrichment.company_size: Small, privately held (sister brand of WrapPixel)
enrichment.seo_title: AdminMart Review: Admin Dashboard Templates for Developers
enrichment.seo_meta_description: AdminMart offers free and premium admin dashboard templates for React, Angular, Vue, and Bootstrap. Compare pricing, features, and use cases before you buy.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: AdminMart is a template marketplace that sells pre-built admin dashboard templates and UI kits aimed at web developers, agencies, and startup founders who need to assemble back-office or SaaS interfaces without designing and coding a dashboard from scratch. Founded in 2018 in Ahmedabad, India, AdminMart operates as a sister brand of WrapPixel, one of the earlier players in the admin-template space, and the two companies share a similar catalog and business model. AdminMart's templates cover a wide range of front-end stacks, including React, Angular, Vue.js, Next.js, Nuxt.js, Bootstrap, Tailwind CSS, and shadcn/ui, so a team can pick a template that matches its existing tech stack rather than adapting its stack to the template. Each template typically ships with ready-made charts, data tables with export to CSV, Excel, and PDF, calendar, chat, email, and invoice application layouts, and more than 100 reusable UI components, cutting down the time needed to stand up an internal tool, admin panel, or SaaS dashboard shell. AdminMart follows a freemium model: many templates (such as the Modernize and FreeDash lines) are available as free, open-source versions on GitHub, while the full-featured premium version of the same template is sold separately, generally priced in the tens of dollars as a one-time purchase rather than a recurring subscription. Premium purchases include one year of support and come with a money-back guarantee, and AdminMart periodically runs bundle promotions (such as Black Friday multi-template packs) that combine several admin and website templates at a discount. Because AdminMart sells design and code assets rather than a hosted service, buyers still need front-end development skills to integrate a template into a real application, connect it to APIs, and customize its branding and business logic; it is not a drag-and-drop dashboard builder or a SaaS platform in its own right. It is best suited for developers and small teams who want a professional-looking, functional dashboard foundation quickly, rather than teams who want a fully managed, no-code admin panel product. AdminMart competes with other template marketplaces like ThemeForest admin categories, CoreUI, and its own sibling WrapPixel, differentiating mainly on price, breadth of framework coverage, and the availability of genuinely free starter versions of its most popular templates.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is AdminMart
paragraphs: AdminMart is an online marketplace for admin dashboard templates and UI kits, built for developers who need a professional-looking back-office interface without designing and coding one from scratch. Rather than being a hosted app or SaaS product, AdminMart sells design and code assets: HTML, CSS, and JavaScript or framework-specific component packages that a developer integrates into their own project.

The company was founded in 2018 in Ahmedabad, India, as a sister brand of WrapPixel, an earlier admin-template company founded by two brothers in 2016. AdminMart and WrapPixel share overlapping catalogs and a similar go-to-market approach, selling both individual templates and multi-template bundles.

AdminMart's catalog spans most major front-end frameworks in use today, including React, Angular, Vue.js, Next.js, Nuxt.js, Bootstrap, Tailwind CSS, and shadcn/ui, so teams can select a template that matches whatever stack they already use in production.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Every AdminMart template bundles a set of common dashboard building blocks: charts (typically via ApexCharts), data tables with export to CSV, Excel, and PDF, calendar views, chat and email inbox layouts, invoicing screens, and authentication pages, giving a project a working baseline on day one.

Templates ship with 100 or more reusable UI components and multiple layout variations (dark and light modes, different sidebar and header styles), which speeds up building out additional screens beyond the included demo pages.

Most popular template families, including Modernize and FreeDash, are offered as free, open-source starter versions on GitHub alongside a paid premium edition with more pages, components, and apps, letting developers evaluate quality before purchasing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: AdminMart uses one-time purchase pricing rather than a recurring subscription for individual templates. Premium templates such as Modernize are commonly priced in the tens of dollars, and purchases include roughly one year of premium support plus a short money-back guarantee window.

Free versions of several flagship templates are available for download and use with fewer pages and components than the premium editions, functioning as both a lead-generation tool and a genuinely usable starter kit for smaller projects.

AdminMart periodically runs bundle promotions, most notably around Black Friday, packaging several admin and website templates together at a reduced combined price compared to buying each one individually.

faqs.answer: AdminMart offers free versions of several of its most popular templates, such as Modernize and FreeDash, with fewer pages and components than the paid premium editions, which are sold as one-time purchases.

faqs.question: What frameworks does AdminMart support?
faqs.answer: AdminMart sells templates for React, Angular, Vue.js, Next.js, Nuxt.js, Bootstrap, Tailwind CSS, and shadcn/ui, covering most major front-end stacks.

faqs.question: Can I use AdminMart templates for client or commercial projects?
faqs.answer: Premium AdminMart licenses generally allow commercial use in client and production projects; specific license terms and seat limits should be checked on the product page before purchase.

faqs.question: Does AdminMart offer refunds?
faqs.answer: AdminMart advertises a short money-back guarantee window on premium template purchases if the product does not work as described.

faqs.question: Is AdminMart a hosted dashboard product?
faqs.answer: No. AdminMart sells design and code templates that developers integrate into their own applications; it does not host or run the dashboard for you.

faqs.question: What is included with a premium AdminMart template?

enrichment.short_description: AdmitSee is a college admissions platform where students search real accepted-student essays and profiles, message peer mentors, and get data-driven insight into what got applicants into their target schools.
enrichment.starting_price: Free limited access for eligible students; paid unlock packages start around 60 dollars
enrichment.seo_title: AdmitSee Review: College Essay and Admissions Data Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: AdmitSee lets students search real accepted-student essays and admissions profiles. See pricing plans, features, pros and cons, and how it compares.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: AdmitSee is a college admissions platform founded in 2013 by former University of Pennsylvania classmates Stephanie Shyu and Lydia Fayal, headquartered in San Francisco with a presence in New York City. The company's core product is a searchable database of real accepted-student application files, including personal statements, supplemental essays, GPA and test scores, extracurricular resumes, honors and awards, and narrative advice about the admissions process, all contributed by students who were accepted to the schools they applied to. The stated mission is to level the admissions playing field by giving high school students affordable access to the kind of insider admissions information that has traditionally been available mainly to families who could afford expensive private counseling. Beyond browsing profiles, AdmitSee lets users message the profile owners directly to ask questions about their application strategy, essay topics, and experience at a given school, effectively functioning as a peer-mentorship layer on top of the data. AdmitSee monetizes through a freemium, unlock-based membership model rather than a flat monthly subscription. A Basic plan sells a set number of profile unlocks (five for 60 dollars) with one year of access, while grade-specific annual memberships, branded by expected graduating class (for example, Class of 2027 through Class of 2030), bundle a larger number of unlocks, messaging privileges, data insights, and advanced search, priced from roughly 150 dollars up to 350 dollars depending on how many years of access and unlocks are included, with per-unlock cost decreasing at higher tiers. AdmitSee also provides free access, typically five profile unlocks, to students attending Title I schools or who qualify for free and reduced lunch programs, targeting under-resourced students directly. For school counselors, a separate Counselor Access plan offers access to 100 profiles and advanced search for a flat annual fee. Beyond the self-serve platform, AdmitSee offers a premium, high-touch advising service pairing students with admissions consultants for a fixed number of hours of one-on-one guidance, priced in the thousands of dollars, positioning the company at both the low-cost, self-serve end and the premium, white-glove end of the college admissions consulting market. AdmitSee is best understood as a data and community product for the college application research phase rather than a full application-management or essay-editing tool, and it competes with other data-driven admissions platforms as well as traditional independent educational consultants.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is AdmitSee
paragraphs: AdmitSee is a college admissions platform built around a searchable database of real accepted-student application files: essays, personal statements, grades, test scores, extracurricular resumes, and admissions advice, all submitted by students who were actually admitted to the schools in question.

Founded in 2013 by former University of Pennsylvania classmates Stephanie Shyu and Lydia Fayal, AdmitSee is headquartered in San Francisco. The company's stated goal is to make admissions information that was traditionally accessible mainly through expensive private counseling available to a much broader group of students.

In addition to browsing profiles, students can message profile owners directly to ask about their essay topics, application strategy, and experience at specific schools, adding a peer-mentorship element on top of the raw data.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The core feature is the searchable database of admitted-student profiles, filterable by school, major, test scores, and other criteria, letting students benchmark their own application against real accepted applicants.
Messaging lets users contact profile owners for direct Q&A, and data insights tools surface aggregate patterns across profiles, such as common essay themes or score ranges for a given school.
AdmitSee also offers a separately priced, higher-touch advising service that pairs students with human admissions consultants for structured one-on-one guidance across the application process.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: AdmitSee uses an unlock-based membership model. A Basic plan provides five profile unlocks for 60 dollars with one year of access, while grade-level annual memberships (Class of 2027 through Class of 2030) bundle more unlocks, messaging, and data insights, ranging from roughly 150 dollars to 350 dollars depending on how many unlocks and years of access are included.

Students attending Title I schools or who qualify for free and reduced lunch programs can receive free access to a limited number of profile unlocks, and school counselors can purchase a dedicated Counselor Access plan covering 100 profiles for a flat annual fee.

For families wanting more direct support, AdmitSee also sells a premium advising package pairing students with a consultant for a fixed number of hours, priced well above the self-serve membership tiers.

faqs.question: What is AdmitSee?
faqs.answer: AdmitSee is a college admissions platform with a searchable database of real accepted-student essays, admissions data, and advice, plus messaging with those admitted students.

faqs.question: How much does AdmitSee cost?
faqs.answer: A Basic plan with five profile unlocks costs 60 dollars, while annual class-based memberships with more unlocks and features range from roughly 150 to 350 dollars depending on tier.

faqs.question: Is AdmitSee free for low-income students?
faqs.answer: Yes, students attending Title I schools or who qualify for free and reduced lunch programs can receive free access to a limited number of profile unlocks.

faqs.question: Can school counselors use AdmitSee?
faqs.answer: Yes, AdmitSee offers a dedicated Counselor Access plan providing access to 100 profiles and advanced search for a flat annual fee.

faqs.question: Does AdmitSee offer human advising, not just data?
faqs.answer: Yes, AdmitSee also sells a premium advising package that pairs students with a consultant for a fixed number of hours of one-on-one guidance.

faqs.question: Who founded AdmitSee?
faqs.answer: AdmitSee was founded in 2013 by former University of Pennsylvania classmates Stephanie Shyu and Lydia Fayal.

faqs.question: Is AdmitSee an essay-editing tool?
faqs.answer: No, AdmitSee's core product is a searchable database of real accepted essays and admissions data for research and benchmarking, not an editing service, though its premium advising add-on can include more hands-on guidance.

enrichment.short_description: Adobe is a publicly traded software giant behind Creative Cloud (Photoshop, Illustrator, Premiere Pro), Acrobat and Document Cloud, and Experience Cloud, serving creative professionals and enterprise marketing teams worldwide.
enrichment.starting_price: Free tier via Adobe Express; paid plans from around 9.99 dollars per month for single apps, with Creative Cloud All Apps around 59.99 dollars per month per user
enrichment.seo_title: Adobe Review: Creative Cloud, Acrobat, and Experience Cloud
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Adobe powers creative, document, and marketing workflows through Creative Cloud, Acrobat, and Experience Cloud. See pricing, features, and use cases.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Adobe Inc. is a publicly traded American software company founded in December 1982 by John Warnock and Charles Geschke, two former Xerox PARC researchers, after they developed the PostScript page description language that helped spark the desktop publishing revolution. Headquartered in San Jose, California, Adobe went public on NASDAQ in 1986 and has grown into one of the largest software companies in the world, with roughly 33,000 employees as of late 2025 and annual revenue in the multi-billion-dollar range. Adobe's business is organized around three major product lines. Creative Cloud is its flagship offering for creative professionals and includes Photoshop for image editing, Illustrator for vector graphics, Premiere Pro and After Effects for video editing and motion graphics, InDesign for page layout, and newer AI-assisted tools like Adobe Express and Firefly for fast content creation. Document Cloud centers on Acrobat, the tool most associated with the PDF format Adobe itself created, offering PDF creation, editing, e-signature (Adobe Sign), and document workflow features for both individuals and enterprises. Experience Cloud is Adobe's enterprise marketing and customer-experience platform, bundling products like Adobe Analytics, Adobe Target, Adobe Campaign, and Adobe Experience Manager for large organizations running data-driven marketing, personalization, and content management at scale. Across these lines, Adobe has moved almost entirely to a subscription model since retiring perpetual-license Creative Suite in favor of Creative Cloud in 2013, with pricing ranging from roughly 9.99 dollars per month for entry-level tools like Adobe Express Premium up to around 59.99 dollars per month per user for the Creative Cloud All Apps bundle for individuals, and higher enterprise pricing negotiated per seat for Experience Cloud and large Creative Cloud deployments. Adobe periodically raises list prices across Creative Cloud, Acrobat, and Experience Cloud, with adjustments in 2026 affecting most VIP Marketplace enterprise agreements. Because this directory entry covers Adobe as a single company and brand rather than any one product, it is best understood as a diversified software and cloud services provider spanning creative tools, document workflows, and enterprise marketing technology, competing across very different markets against tools like Canva and Figma in creative software, DocuSign and Foxit in document workflows, and Salesforce and HubSpot in marketing technology.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Adobe
paragraphs: Adobe Inc. is a publicly traded American software company founded in December 1982 by John Warnock and Charles Geschke, former Xerox PARC researchers who built the PostScript page description language, a technology that helped launch the desktop publishing revolution and gave Adobe its start.

Headquartered in San Jose, California, Adobe went public on NASDAQ in 1986 and has since grown into one of the largest software companies in the world, employing roughly 33,000 people as of late 2025 and generating multi-billion-dollar annual revenue.

Adobe's business spans three main areas: Creative Cloud for creative professionals, Document Cloud built around Acrobat and PDF workflows, and Experience Cloud for enterprise marketing and customer-experience technology.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Creative Cloud includes flagship applications such as Photoshop, Illustrator, Premiere Pro, After Effects, and InDesign, alongside newer AI-driven tools like Adobe Express for quick social and marketing content and Firefly for generative image and design assistance.

Document Cloud is centered on Acrobat, offering PDF creation, editing, commenting, and e-signature workflows through Adobe Sign, used broadly by both individual professionals and large enterprises for document processes.

Experience Cloud bundles enterprise marketing products, including Adobe Analytics, Adobe Target, Adobe Campaign, and Adobe Experience Manager, aimed at large organizations that need data-driven personalization, analytics, and content management at scale.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Adobe operates almost entirely on subscription pricing since replacing perpetual-license Creative Suite with Creative Cloud in 2013. Entry-level tools such as Adobe Express Premium start around 9.99 dollars per month, while single Creative Cloud apps run higher, and the Creative Cloud All Apps bundle for individuals is priced around 59.99 dollars per month per user.

Document Cloud (Acrobat) plans start at roughly 12.99 dollars per month per user for Standard and scale up to around 19.99 dollars per month for Pro, with enterprise pricing negotiated based on seat count.

Experience Cloud pricing is fully custom, based on factors like data volume, page views, API calls, and implementation scope, and is sold through direct enterprise sales rather than a public price list. Adobe has periodically increased list prices across all three product lines, including adjustments effective in 2026.

Continuous subscription updates deliver new AI and workflow features without separate upgrade purchases
Strong enterprise support, training resources, and a large ecosystem of tutorials and third-party plugins
Cross-application integration lets creative and marketing teams share assets and workflows across products
cons: Subscription costs add up quickly for teams needing multiple Creative Cloud or Experience Cloud products

Learning curve for full-featured tools like Photoshop and Premiere Pro can be steep for casual users
Cheaper, simpler competitors (Canva, DocuSign, Figma) have eroded Adobe's position in some lighter-weight use cases

faqs.question: What is Adobe best known for?
faqs.answer: Adobe is best known for Creative Cloud applications like Photoshop, Illustrator, and Premiere Pro, as well as Acrobat and the PDF format, and enterprise marketing technology under Experience Cloud.

faqs.question: Is Adobe a public company?
faqs.answer: Yes, Adobe Inc. has been publicly traded on NASDAQ since 1986 under the ticker ADBE.

faqs.question: How much does Adobe Creative Cloud cost?
faqs.answer: Pricing ranges from about 9.99 dollars per month for entry tools like Adobe Express Premium to around 59.99 dollars per month per user for the Creative Cloud All Apps bundle, with enterprise pricing negotiated separately.

faqs.question: Does Adobe still sell perpetual software licenses?
faqs.answer: No, Adobe moved almost entirely to subscription pricing when it replaced Creative Suite with Creative Cloud in 2013.

faqs.question: What is Adobe Experience Cloud?
faqs.answer: Experience Cloud is Adobe's enterprise marketing and customer-experience platform, including Adobe Analytics, Adobe Target, Adobe Campaign, and Adobe Experience Manager, sold with custom enterprise pricing.

faqs.question: Who founded Adobe?
faqs.answer: Adobe was founded in December 1982 by John Warnock and Charles Geschke, former Xerox PARC researchers, after they developed the PostScript page description language.

faqs.question: Where is Adobe headquartered?
faqs.answer: Adobe is headquartered in San Jose, California.

faqs.question: Does Adobe offer free tools?
faqs.answer: Yes, Adobe offers free tiers such as Adobe Express, along with free trials of most Creative Cloud and Document Cloud subscription products.

enrichment.short_description: AdRoll is a cross-channel retargeting and programmatic advertising platform for ecommerce brands, running display, social, and email campaigns from one dashboard.
enrichment.starting_price: Starter self-service plan from around 36 dollars per month plus ad spend
enrichment.seo_title: AdRoll Review: Retargeting and Cross-Channel Ad Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: AdRoll is a retargeting and cross-channel advertising platform for ecommerce. See pricing plans, features, pros and cons, and how it works.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: AdRoll is a retargeting and cross-channel advertising platform built primarily for ecommerce brands, founded in 2007 as a display retargeting specialist and now operating as a division of NextRoll, headquartered in San Francisco, California. The platform has expanded well beyond its original display-retargeting roots into a broader advertising tool covering display ads, social advertising on channels like Meta, TikTok, and Pinterest, and email marketing, positioned around unifying customer data and campaign execution across channels from a single dashboard. AdRoll's core value proposition is helping ecommerce merchants re-engage site visitors and past customers who did not convert on their first visit, using pixel-based tracking and audience segmentation to serve targeted ads across the open web and major social platforms, alongside prospecting tools to find new, similar customers. The platform integrates with major ecommerce platforms such as Shopify, BigCommerce, and WooCommerce, making it straightforward for online stores to sync product catalogs and customer data without custom development work. AdRoll's pricing combines a platform subscription fee with media spend: its Self-Service tier is pay-as-you-go with no minimum spend requirement, letting advertisers start campaigns with any budget and pay per impression via a dynamic CPM model, with cross-channel attribution and social ads available as add-ons. Its Managed Service tier requires a qualifying level of ad spend and adds dedicated account support, guaranteed onboarding, and free ad credits, typically requiring an annual commitment for the top Advanced package. Published pricing references indicate a Starter self-service plan around 36 dollars per month, a Growth plan around 67 dollars per month, and custom Enterprise pricing, on top of a margin AdRoll typically takes on underlying ad spend running through the platform (commonly cited in the 15 to 20 percent range). AdRoll also offers dedicated account-based marketing (ABM) packages, including account-based advertising, account-based marketing plus advertising, and account-based retargeting, the last of which can start free on a pay-per-media basis. AdRoll is best suited for small to mid-size ecommerce businesses that want an accessible, self-serve retargeting and cross-channel ad tool without needing a dedicated in-house programmatic advertising team, and it competes with tools like Criteo, Google Ads remarketing, and native retargeting features within Meta and TikTok ad managers.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is AdRoll
paragraphs: AdRoll is a retargeting and cross-channel advertising platform originally launched in 2007 as a specialist in display retargeting, the practice of showing ads to people who previously visited a website but did not convert.

Over time, AdRoll expanded from pure display retargeting into a broader advertising platform covering display, social ads on Meta, TikTok, and Pinterest, and email, aimed primarily at ecommerce brands managing customer acquisition and re-engagement across multiple channels.

AdRoll is headquartered in San Francisco, California, and operates as a division of NextRoll, the parent company formed from AdRoll's evolution and expansion into adjacent advertising technology products.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: AdRoll's core feature is pixel-based retargeting, which tracks site visitors and serves them targeted display and social ads based on their browsing behavior, alongside prospecting tools that find new audiences similar to existing customers.

The platform integrates directly with major ecommerce platforms like Shopify, BigCommerce, and WooCommerce, syncing product catalogs and customer data automatically to power dynamic product ads without custom development.

Cross-channel attribution reporting and email marketing tools are available as add-ons, letting merchants track how display, social, and email campaigns work together across the customer journey from a single dashboard.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: AdRoll's Self-Service tier is pay-as-you-go with no minimum spend requirement, charging a dynamic CPM on ad delivery plus a platform fee, with published references citing a Starter plan around 36 dollars per month and a Growth plan around 67 dollars per month.

The Managed Service tier requires a qualifying level of ad spend and adds dedicated account management, guaranteed onboarding, and free ad credits, with the top-tier Advanced package typically requiring an annual commitment and custom, sales-negotiated pricing.

On top of platform fees, AdRoll generally takes a margin on the underlying media spend running through the platform, commonly cited in the 15 to 20 percent range, and offers separate account-based marketing (ABM) packages priced by tier, including a free-to-start, pay-per-media account-based retargeting option.

features.title: Pixel-based retargeting
features.description: Track site visitors and re-engage them with targeted display and social ads based on browsing behavior.

features.title: Cross-channel campaigns
features.description: Run display, social ads on Meta, TikTok, and Pinterest, and email campaigns from a single platform.

features.title: Ecommerce platform integrations
features.description: Native integrations with Shopify, BigCommerce, and WooCommerce sync product catalogs and customer data automatically.

features.title: Dynamic product ads
features.description: Automatically generate personalized ad creative based on products a shopper viewed or abandoned in cart.

features.title: Cross-channel attribution
features.description: Optional add-on reporting that shows how display, social, and email campaigns contribute to conversions together.

features.title: Prospecting and lookalike audiences
features.description: Find new customers similar to existing high-value customers using AdRoll's audience data.

features.title: Self-service and managed service tiers

Native integrations with Shopify, BigCommerce, and WooCommerce simplify setup
Covers display, social, and email from one platform rather than juggling separate tools
Managed service tier offers dedicated support for merchants who want hands-off campaign management
Dedicated ABM packages extend the platform to B2B account-based marketing use cases

Best results generally require meaningful site traffic and spend, limiting value for very small stores
Some cross-channel features (attribution, social ads) are gated as paid add-ons rather than included by default

faqs.question: What is AdRoll used for?
faqs.answer: AdRoll is used to run retargeting and cross-channel advertising campaigns, showing ads to past site visitors and prospective customers across display, social, and email.

faqs.question: How much does AdRoll cost?
faqs.answer: AdRoll's self-service tier starts around 36 dollars per month for Starter and around 67 dollars per month for Growth, plus ad spend and a margin on media; Enterprise and Managed Service pricing require contacting sales.

faqs.question: Does AdRoll have a minimum ad spend?
faqs.answer: No, AdRoll's self-service plan has no minimum spend requirement, though its Managed Service tier requires a qualifying level of ad spend.

faqs.question: What ecommerce platforms does AdRoll integrate with?
faqs.answer: AdRoll integrates natively with major ecommerce platforms including Shopify, BigCommerce, and WooCommerce.

faqs.question: Who owns AdRoll?
faqs.answer: AdRoll operates as a division of NextRoll, its parent company.

faqs.question: Can AdRoll run social media ads?
faqs.answer: Yes, AdRoll supports social advertising on channels including Meta, TikTok, and Pinterest, in addition to its original display retargeting.

faqs.question: Is AdRoll good for B2B account-based marketing?
faqs.answer: AdRoll offers dedicated account-based marketing (ABM) packages, including account-based advertising and retargeting tiers, for B2B use cases.

enrichment.short_description: AdStage was a cross-network PPC campaign management and reporting platform that pivoted to ad analytics before being acquired by TapClicks in 2020; the standalone platform is no longer sold, and adstage.io now runs as a free PPC tools and content hub.
enrichment.company_size: Small (formerly around 24 employees at acquisition; now part of TapClicks)
enrichment.headquarters: San Francisco, California, United States (originally); now part of TapClicks, headquartered in Santa Clara, California
enrichment.seo_title: AdStage Review: History, Acquisition by TapClicks, and Current Status
enrichment.seo_meta_description: AdStage was a PPC campaign management and analytics platform acquired by TapClicks in 2020. See its history, what happened to it, and current status.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: AdStage was a cross-network advertising campaign management platform founded in San Francisco around 2013 by Sahil Jain and Jason Wu, originally built to let digital marketers build, manage, and optimize paid search and social campaigns across networks like Google Ads, Bing, Facebook, and LinkedIn from one interface. Over its life, AdStage raised roughly 15.2 million dollars in venture funding, including a 3 million dollar strategic round in March 2018 backed by Forte Ventures, HubSpot, and Verizon Ventures. Around 2018, AdStage shifted its positioning away from being a campaign-management tool toward becoming a campaign analytics, reporting, and automation platform, reporting 150 percent revenue growth after the pivot. In April 2020, marketing operations company TapClicks acquired AdStage to fold its marketing intelligence and predictive campaign optimization capabilities into TapClicks' broader Unified Marketing Platform, joining other TapClicks-owned brands such as Raven Tools, iSpionage, and Megalytic. As a result, AdStage no longer exists as an independently sold SaaS product; its reporting and analytics technology has been absorbed into TapClicks' platform, and prospective buyers looking for what AdStage used to offer should now evaluate TapClicks directly. The adstage.io domain remains live but now functions primarily as a content and free-tools hub for PPC marketers, publishing blog content on paid advertising topics (such as Meta and Threads ad updates) and offering free utilities like ad copy generators and keyword checkers, rather than operating as a paid subscription reporting platform. Because the original standalone AdStage product is discontinued and its functionality now lives inside TapClicks' enterprise offering, there is no current public self-serve pricing for AdStage as a distinct product; anyone evaluating it for procurement should treat it as legacy/historical and instead engage with TapClicks for current pricing and capabilities. This entry is included for completeness and historical accuracy: AdStage is not a live, independently purchasable SaaS tool as of 2026, even though its brand and web presence persist in a reduced, content-focused form.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Was AdStage
paragraphs: AdStage was a cross-network advertising campaign management platform founded around 2013 in San Francisco by Sahil Jain and Jason Wu, built to let digital marketers build, manage, and optimize paid search and social campaigns across multiple ad networks, including Google Ads, Bing, Facebook, and LinkedIn, from a single interface.

The company raised approximately 15.2 million dollars in venture funding over its life, including a 3 million dollar strategic round in March 2018 backed by Forte Ventures, HubSpot, and Verizon Ventures, and reported 150 percent revenue growth after pivoting from campaign management toward analytics and reporting around 2018.

In April 2020, AdStage was acquired by TapClicks, a marketing operations and reporting company, which folded AdStage's analytics and optimization technology into its own Unified Marketing Platform alongside other acquired brands like Raven Tools and Megalytic.

long_form_blocks.heading: What AdStage Offered (Historical)
paragraphs: At its peak, AdStage offered self-serve cross-network campaign management and reporting, letting marketers build and adjust ad campaigns across search and social networks, then automate rule-based optimizations for bidding and budget pacing.
Its analytics and reporting features aggregated performance data across multiple ad platforms into unified dashboards, a capability that TapClicks specifically acquired the company for in 2020.

Today, the adstage.io site retains only a lightweight version of this legacy in the form of free PPC utilities such as ad copy generators and keyword checkers, plus editorial content on paid advertising trends, rather than the original paid SaaS reporting product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Current Status and Pricing
paragraphs: AdStage is not currently sold as a standalone product. Following its April 2020 acquisition by TapClicks, its reporting and optimization technology was integrated into TapClicks' broader Unified Marketing Platform, which is sold under the TapClicks brand with its own custom, sales-negotiated enterprise pricing.
There is no public self-serve pricing page for AdStage as an independent tool, since the platform it referred to no longer operates separately from TapClicks.

The adstage.io website remains active but now functions as a free content and tools hub rather than a paid subscription product, so anyone evaluating this listing should understand it primarily as historical context rather than an actively purchasable SaaS platform.

features.title: Cross-network campaign management (historical)
features.description: Originally let marketers manage paid search and social campaigns across Google Ads, Bing, Facebook, and LinkedIn from one interface.

features.title: Automated campaign optimization (historical)
features.description: Rule-based automation for bid and budget adjustments across connected ad networks.

features.title: Cross-channel reporting (historical)
features.description: Aggregated performance data across multiple ad platforms into unified dashboards, later the basis for its TapClicks acquisition.

features.title: Free PPC tools (current)
features.description: The adstage.io site now offers free utilities such as ad copy generators and keyword checkers.

features.title: PPC industry content (current)
features.description: Ongoing blog coverage of paid advertising platform changes and trends, such as Meta and Threads ad updates.

features.title: Integration into TapClicks
features.description: AdStage's analytics and optimization capabilities now live inside TapClicks' Unified Marketing Platform rather than as a separate product.

pros: Historically offered genuinely useful cross-network campaign management for SMB marketers
Its analytics technology lives on, now backed by TapClicks' broader resources and platform
Current adstage.io site still offers some free, no-cost PPC utilities and educational content
Well documented acquisition history provides transparency about what happened to the product
cons: The original standalone AdStage SaaS product is no longer sold; it is not a live, purchasable tool
No public pricing exists for AdStage today since its capabilities were folded into TapClicks
Buyers researching AdStage for procurement may be misled by the still-active domain into thinking it is a current product
Anyone wanting AdStage's original functionality must now evaluate and purchase TapClicks instead

faqs.question: Is AdStage still available as a product?
faqs.answer: No, AdStage is no longer sold as a standalone product. It was acquired by TapClicks in April 2020, and its analytics and campaign optimization technology was folded into TapClicks' Unified Marketing Platform.

faqs.question: Who acquired AdStage?
faqs.answer: TapClicks, a marketing operations and reporting company, acquired AdStage in April 2020 to add marketing intelligence and predictive campaign optimization to its platform.

faqs.question: What does adstage.io show today?
faqs.answer: The adstage.io domain remains active as a free content and tools hub for PPC marketers, offering blog articles and free utilities like ad copy generators, rather than the original paid campaign management platform.

faqs.question: When was AdStage founded and by whom?
faqs.answer: AdStage was founded around 2013 in San Francisco by Sahil Jain and Jason Wu.

faqs.question: What did AdStage originally do?
faqs.answer: AdStage originally let marketers build, manage, and optimize paid search and social campaigns across networks like Google Ads, Bing, Facebook, and LinkedIn from one platform, later pivoting to focus on analytics and reporting.

faqs.question: How much funding did AdStage raise?
faqs.answer: AdStage raised approximately 15.2 million dollars in venture funding over its life, including a 3 million dollar round in March 2018.

faqs.question: Where can I find AdStage's current pricing?
faqs.answer: There is no current standalone pricing for AdStage; its capabilities are now part of TapClicks' Unified Marketing Platform, which uses custom enterprise pricing.

enrichment.short_description: Advent Software was a pioneering investment management software company acquired by SS&C Technologies in 2015; its portfolio accounting, trading, and reporting products now operate as SS&C Advent within SS&C's platform.
enrichment.company_size: Large (part of SS&C Technologies, a global enterprise)
enrichment.headquarters: Formerly San Francisco, California; now part of SS&C Technologies, headquartered in Windsor, Connecticut
enrichment.seo_title: Advent Software Review: History and SS&C Advent Today
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Advent Software was acquired by SS&C Technologies in 2015 and now operates as SS&C Advent. See its history, products, and current status.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Advent Software was an investment management software company founded in San Francisco in June 1983 by Stephanie DiMarco and Steve Strand, built to serve investment managers, hedge funds, family offices, and wealth managers with portfolio accounting, trading, compliance, and reporting technology. Advent built an early reputation on innovation in the space: in 1984 it launched one of the first PC-based portfolio accounting systems, and in 1992 it introduced Axys, an early Windows-based portfolio accounting and reporting solution that became widely used across the buy-side. The company later developed Advent Geneva, a global portfolio management and accounting platform used by hedge funds and asset managers, among other products. Advent went public on NASDAQ under the ticker ADVS and grew into one of the most established names in investment management technology over more than three decades as an independent company. On February 2, 2015, SS&C Technologies Holdings announced a definitive agreement to acquire Advent Software for an enterprise value of approximately 2.7 billion dollars in cash, equating to 44.25 dollars per share plus assumption of debt; the acquisition closed on July 8, 2015. Following the deal, the combined company became a leading global provider of investment management software and services, serving more than 10,000 customers worldwide with combined revenues of approximately 1.2 billion dollars at the time. Advent's products were rebranded as SS&C Advent, a business unit within SS&C Technologies, and continue to be sold and supported under that name today rather than as an independent company. SS&C Advent's current offerings, organized by functional category rather than legacy product names, span portfolio management, accounting and reporting, trading, rebalancing and compliance, fund order management, data management, client portals, fund accounting, investor servicing, margin and finance management, research management, client relationship management, and performance, reporting and attribution, serving more than 5,000 buy-side firms and reportedly supporting management of trillions of dollars in assets. This directory entry reflects the historical Advent Software brand; buyers evaluating this technology today should understand it as SS&C Advent, a division of SS&C Technologies headquartered in Windsor, Connecticut, sold with custom enterprise pricing rather than as a separately purchasable independent company.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Was Advent Software
paragraphs: Advent Software was an investment management software company founded in San Francisco in June 1983 by Stephanie DiMarco and Steve Strand, built to provide portfolio accounting, trading, compliance, and reporting technology to investment managers, hedge funds, family offices, and wealth managers.

The company built its early reputation on genuine firsts in the space: it launched one of the first PC-based portfolio accounting systems in 1984, and introduced Axys, an early Windows-based portfolio accounting and reporting solution, in 1992. It later developed Advent Geneva, a global portfolio management platform used widely by hedge funds and asset managers.
Advent traded publicly on NASDAQ under the ticker ADVS for many years and became one of the most established, independently operated names in investment management technology before its acquisition.

long_form_blocks.heading: Acquisition by SS&C and Current Status
paragraphs: On February 2, 2015, SS&C Technologies Holdings announced an agreement to acquire Advent Software for an enterprise value of approximately 2.7 billion dollars in cash, or 44.25 dollars per share plus assumption of debt, with the acquisition completing on July 8, 2015.

Following the acquisition, Advent's product lines were rebranded as SS&C Advent and continue to operate as a business unit within SS&C Technologies rather than as an independent company, with the combined entity serving more than 10,000 customers worldwide at the time of the deal.

SS&C Advent today organizes its offerings by functional capability rather than legacy Advent product names, spanning portfolio management, accounting and reporting, trading, compliance, fund order management, data management, client portals, and investor servicing, among other areas.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: SS&C Advent, like most enterprise investment management software, is sold through direct enterprise sales with custom pricing based on assets under management, number of users, and which modules a firm licenses (portfolio accounting, trading, compliance, reporting, and so on).

There is no public self-serve pricing available for SS&C Advent products; prospective buyers, typically hedge funds, asset managers, and wealth management firms, engage directly with SS&C's sales team for quotes and implementation scoping.

Because the platform now spans SS&C's broader investment management technology portfolio, pricing can also depend on whether a firm licenses SS&C Advent modules alongside other SS&C products as part of a broader platform deal.

features.description: Core investment accounting and portfolio management tools built on Advent's decades of buy-side software experience.

features.description: Tools for executing trades, rebalancing portfolios, and enforcing compliance rules across investment mandates.

features.title: Fund order management
features.description: Order management workflows tailored for fund managers handling multiple accounts and strategies.

features.title: Client portal and investor servicing
features.description: Client-facing reporting and portal tools for investment managers to communicate with their own clients and investors.

features.title: Performance, reporting and attribution
features.description: Tools for calculating and reporting portfolio performance and attribution across strategies and benchmarks.

features.title: Data management
features.description: Centralized data management capabilities that feed accounting, trading, and reporting workflows consistently.

features.title: Research and client relationship management
features.description: Modules supporting investment research workflows and CRM functions for client-facing investment teams.

pros: Deep, decades-long track record in investment management software, dating to 1983
Backed by SS&C Technologies' scale and resources following the 2015 acquisition
Broad functional coverage spanning accounting, trading, compliance, and reporting in one ecosystem
Serves a large, established base of more than 5,000 buy-side firms globally
Continuity of legacy products like Geneva under a larger, well-capitalized parent company
cons: No longer an independent company, so buyers are evaluating SS&C's broader offering rather than a standalone Advent product
Pricing is entirely custom and not published, requiring a sales engagement to get a quote
Enterprise-grade investment management platforms typically involve significant implementation time and cost
Legacy product naming can be confusing since offerings are now organized by SS&C's functional categories rather than original Advent product names

faqs.question: Does Advent Software still exist as an independent company?
faqs.answer: No, Advent Software was acquired by SS&C Technologies in 2015 and now operates as SS&C Advent, a business unit within SS&C rather than an independent company.

faqs.question: When was Advent Software founded?
faqs.answer: Advent Software was founded in San Francisco in June 1983 by Stephanie DiMarco and Steve Strand.

faqs.question: When and for how much was Advent Software acquired?
faqs.answer: SS&C Technologies acquired Advent Software in a deal announced February 2, 2015 and completed July 8, 2015, for an enterprise value of approximately 2.7 billion dollars, or 44.25 dollars per share plus assumed debt.

faqs.question: What products did Advent Software make?
faqs.answer: Advent's notable products included Axys, one of the first Windows-based portfolio accounting systems, and Advent Geneva, a global portfolio management platform used by hedge funds and asset managers.

faqs.question: What is SS&C Advent?
faqs.answer: SS&C Advent is the current name for Advent Software's product lines, organized as a business unit within SS&C Technologies, offering portfolio management, trading, compliance, and reporting tools for investment managers.

faqs.question: How much does SS&C Advent cost?
faqs.answer: SS&C Advent uses custom enterprise pricing based on assets under management, user count, and licensed modules; there is no public self-serve pricing.

faqs.question: Who uses SS&C Advent today?
faqs.answer: SS&C Advent serves more than 5,000 buy-side firms globally, including hedge funds, asset managers, family offices, and wealth managers.

enrichment.short_description: AdwCleaner is a free, portable Windows utility from Malwarebytes that scans and removes adware, browser hijackers, toolbars, and other potentially unwanted programs.
enrichment.headquarters: Santa Clara, California, United States (Malwarebytes headquarters)
enrichment.seo_title: AdwCleaner Review: Free Adware and PUP Removal Tool
enrichment.seo_meta_description: AdwCleaner is a free Malwarebytes tool that removes adware, browser hijackers, and unwanted programs from Windows. See features, pros, cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: AdwCleaner is a free, portable Windows security utility originally developed by the French software company ToolsLib (also associated with founders Jerome Boursier and Corentin Chepeau) and acquired by Malwarebytes in October 2016. The tool is purpose-built to detect and remove adware, potentially unwanted programs (PUPs), browser hijackers, and unwanted toolbars, categories of low-grade but persistent nuisance software that traditional antivirus engines do not always prioritize. At the time of its acquisition, AdwCleaner had already been installed more than 200 million times and was averaging over 200,000 downloads per day, reflecting strong organic adoption among both everyday consumers and IT technicians who use it as a go-to cleanup tool for infected machines. AdwCleaner requires no installation: users download a single executable, run it directly (including from a USB drive), and it scans the system for adware, PUPs, invalid shortcuts, suspicious registry entries, and browser hijacker artifacts, then presents a list of detected items for the user to review and remove. Its interface is deliberately simple, aimed at users who want a fast, low-friction cleanup rather than deep antivirus configuration. Since the Malwarebytes acquisition, AdwCleaner has remained a free, standalone product rather than being merged fully into paid Malwarebytes Premium, continuing to be distributed both through Malwarebytes' own site and third-party download repositories like Bleeping Computer, MajorGeeks, and ToolsLib. It is commonly used as a complement to, not a replacement for, full antivirus or anti-malware software, since it specifically targets adware and PUP-class threats rather than viruses, ransomware, or more sophisticated malware. Because it is free with no paid tier, AdwCleaner's business model is best understood as a customer acquisition and goodwill tool for Malwarebytes, whose broader business is built on paid Malwarebytes Premium subscriptions for real-time protection; AdwCleaner itself carries no subscription, license fee, or premium version. It remains a widely recommended tool in Windows troubleshooting communities and tech support forums for quickly cleaning up adware infections and unwanted browser changes.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is AdwCleaner
paragraphs: AdwCleaner is a free, portable Windows utility that scans for and removes adware, potentially unwanted programs (PUPs), browser hijackers, and unwanted toolbars, categories of intrusive but low-severity software that clutter browsers and slow down PCs.

The tool was originally developed by the French software company ToolsLib and was acquired by Malwarebytes in October 2016, with two of its original developers, Jerome Boursier and Corentin Chepeau, joining Malwarebytes in engineering and research roles as part of the deal.

By the time of its acquisition, AdwCleaner had already been installed more than 200 million times and averaged over 200,000 downloads per day, making it one of the most widely used dedicated adware-removal tools for Windows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: AdwCleaner requires no installation: users download a single executable and run it directly, including from a USB drive, making it convenient for IT technicians cleaning up multiple machines.

The scanner detects adware, PUPs, invalid shortcuts, suspicious registry entries, and browser hijacker artifacts, presenting a clear list of detected items that the user can review and remove selectively.
Because AdwCleaner focuses specifically on adware and PUP-class threats rather than viruses or ransomware, it is typically used alongside, not instead of, a full antivirus or anti-malware product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: AdwCleaner is completely free, with no premium tier, subscription, or license fee for the standalone tool itself.

Since it is owned by Malwarebytes, AdwCleaner functions in part as a customer acquisition tool that can introduce users to Malwarebytes' paid Malwarebytes Premium subscription, which provides real-time protection against malware, ransomware, and other threats beyond adware and PUPs.
There is no indication that Malwarebytes plans to charge for AdwCleaner itself; it remains distributed free both from Malwarebytes' own site and third-party download repositories.

features.title: Adware and PUP removal
features.description: Scans for and removes adware and potentially unwanted programs bundled with other software installs.

features.title: Browser hijacker cleanup
features.description: Detects and removes browser hijackers that alter homepages, search engines, or new tab settings without consent.

features.title: Toolbar cleaner
features.description: Removes unwanted browser toolbars and extensions installed alongside other downloads.

features.title: Portable, no-install execution
features.description: Runs as a single executable with no installation required, usable directly from a USB drive.

features.title: Fast scan times
features.description: Completes a full system scan in a short amount of time compared to full antivirus suites.

features.title: Simple review-and-remove workflow
features.description: Presents detected items in a clear list for the user to review before removal, avoiding accidental deletions.

features.title: Free, standalone distribution
features.description: Distributed at no cost directly through Malwarebytes and trusted third-party download sites.

pros: Completely free with no premium tier or subscription required
No installation needed, making it fast and convenient for one-off cleanups
Backed by Malwarebytes, a well-established name in consumer security software
Widely trusted and recommended across tech support communities and forums
Focused, lightweight tool that does not conflict with existing antivirus software
cons: Not a substitute for full antivirus or anti-malware protection against viruses and ransomware
Focused narrowly on adware and PUP-class threats, so it will not catch more sophisticated malware
Windows-only, with no macOS or Linux version available
Manual, on-demand scanning only; it does not provide real-time or scheduled protection

faqs.question: Is AdwCleaner really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, AdwCleaner is completely free with no premium tier, subscription, or license fee.

faqs.question: Who owns AdwCleaner?
faqs.answer: AdwCleaner was originally developed by ToolsLib and has been owned by Malwarebytes since Malwarebytes acquired it in October 2016.

faqs.question: Does AdwCleaner replace antivirus software?
faqs.answer: No, AdwCleaner is designed to complement, not replace, full antivirus or anti-malware software; it specifically targets adware, PUPs, and browser hijackers rather than viruses or ransomware.

faqs.question: Do I need to install AdwCleaner?
faqs.answer: No, AdwCleaner is a portable executable that runs directly without installation, including from a USB drive.

faqs.question: What operating systems does AdwCleaner support?
faqs.answer: AdwCleaner is a Windows-only utility; it does not have macOS or Linux versions.

faqs.question: How often should I run AdwCleaner?
faqs.answer: AdwCleaner is designed for on-demand scanning, typically run when a system shows signs of adware or unwanted browser changes, rather than on a fixed schedule.

faqs.question: Is AdwCleaner safe to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, AdwCleaner is a legitimate, widely used tool from Malwarebytes; it presents detected items for user review before removal to avoid accidental deletions.

use_cases.title: Cleaning up an adware-infected PC
use_cases.description: Everyday users run AdwCleaner to remove adware, toolbars, and browser hijackers that slow down or clutter their computer.

use_cases.title: IT technician cleanup workflows
use_cases.description: IT support technicians use AdwCleaner as a fast, portable tool to clean up client and customer machines during service visits.

use_cases.title: Complementary layer to antivirus software
use_cases.description: Security-conscious users run AdwCleaner alongside their existing antivirus software to catch adware and PUPs that antivirus tools may deprioritize.

features: Adware and PUP scanning and removal

enrichment.short_description: Affinity is a relationship intelligence CRM for venture capital, private equity, and investment banking teams that automatically captures email and calendar data to surface warm introductions and manage deal flow.
enrichment.starting_price: Roughly 2,000 dollars per user per year, with an approximate 20,000 dollar annual minimum
enrichment.seo_title: Affinity Review: Relationship Intelligence CRM for VC and Private Equity
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Affinity is a relationship intelligence CRM built for venture capital and private equity dealmakers. See pricing, features, pros and cons, and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Affinity is a relationship intelligence CRM built specifically for private capital dealmakers, including venture capital firms, private equity firms, and investment banks, headquartered in San Francisco and founded in 2014. Unlike general-purpose CRMs, Affinity's core differentiator is automated relationship-data capture: rather than requiring analysts and partners to manually log contacts and interactions, Affinity ingests email and calendar activity across a firm's team to automatically build a network graph of relationships, reportedly spanning more than 50 million people, without manual data entry. This lets firms see who on their team has the strongest existing relationship with a target company's founders or executives, surfacing warm introduction paths for sourcing new deals rather than relying on cold outreach. Affinity also enriches company profiles automatically with data such as funding history and revenue signals, and provides relationship scoring that ranks the strength of connections to help partners prioritize outreach. The platform is used across the full private capital lifecycle: deal sourcing and pipeline management, relationship and network management, and portfolio or investor relations tracking after a deal closes. Affinity has raised institutional venture funding itself, including an 80 million dollar Series C round, and counts investors such as Menlo Ventures, Advance Venture Partners, 8VC, and MassMutual Ventures among its backers; the company reports serving more than 3,000 customers across more than 70 countries. Pricing is not published on a self-serve basis; industry pricing references suggest costs of roughly 2,000 dollars or more per user per year, with an approximate 20,000 dollar annual minimum contract value, positioning Affinity firmly as an enterprise product for professional investment firms rather than a low-cost, self-serve CRM. It should not be confused with Affinity Photo or Affinity Designer, the unrelated creative software products made by Serif; this Affinity, found at affinity.co, is a relationship-intelligence and deal-management platform purpose-built for capital markets, venture capital, and private equity workflows, competing with tools like DealCloud, Pitchbook's CRM features, and Salesforce-based deal-tracking implementations customized for finance.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Affinity
paragraphs: Affinity is a relationship intelligence CRM built specifically for private capital dealmakers, including venture capital firms, private equity firms, and investment banks. Founded in 2014 and headquartered in San Francisco, Affinity focuses on the unique deal-sourcing and relationship-management needs of investment professionals rather than general sales teams.

The company's defining feature is automated relationship-data capture: Affinity ingests email and calendar activity across a firm's team to automatically build a network graph, reportedly covering more than 50 million people, without requiring manual data entry from analysts or partners.

Affinity is distinct from Affinity Photo or Affinity Designer, the unrelated creative software products from Serif; the affinity.co product covered here is purpose-built for capital markets and dealmaking workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Automated relationship capture is Affinity's core feature, analyzing email and calendar activity to surface who on a firm's team has the strongest existing relationship with a target company's founders or executives, revealing warm introduction paths for new deals.

Company profile enrichment automatically pulls in data like funding history and revenue signals, reducing manual research when evaluating a potential deal, while relationship scoring ranks the strength of connections to help partners prioritize outreach.

Affinity supports the full private capital lifecycle: deal sourcing and pipeline management, relationship and network management across the firm, and post-close portfolio or investor relations tracking, all within one platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Affinity does not publish self-serve pricing; costs are quoted directly by the sales team based on firm size, module needs, and contract length.

Industry pricing references indicate costs of roughly 2,000 dollars or more per user per year, with an approximate 20,000 dollar annual minimum contract value, positioning Affinity as an enterprise-grade tool for professional investment firms rather than a low-cost, self-serve CRM.

Because pricing scales with team size and the modules a firm licenses (CRM, relationship intelligence, portfolio monitoring), total cost varies significantly between a small VC fund and a large private equity firm.

features.title: Automated relationship capture
features.description: Analyzes team email and calendar data to build a network graph without manual data entry.

features.title: Warm introduction discovery
features.description: Surfaces which team member has the strongest relationship with a target company's decision-makers.

features.title: Company profile enrichment
features.description: Automatically enriches company records with funding history, revenue signals, and other deal-relevant data.

features.title: Relationship scoring
features.description: Ranks the strength of relationships to help partners prioritize outreach and follow-up.

features.title: Deal pipeline management
features.description: Tracks deals through sourcing, diligence, and close stages tailored to private capital workflows.

features.title: Portfolio and investor relations tracking
features.description: Supports post-close relationship management with portfolio companies and limited partners.

features.title: Network-wide visibility
features.description: Gives an entire firm visibility into its collective network rather than siloed, individual contact lists.

features.title: Integrations with email and calendar systems
features.description: Connects to firm email and calendar platforms to power its automated data capture without manual logging.

pros: Automated relationship capture removes the manual data-entry burden common in traditional CRMs
Purpose-built for venture capital, private equity, and investment banking dealmaking workflows
Warm-introduction discovery can meaningfully improve deal sourcing versus cold outreach
Backed by significant venture funding and a large customer base across 70-plus countries
Combines CRM, relationship intelligence, and portfolio tracking in one integrated platform
cons: Pricing is not published and is positioned at an enterprise price point, roughly 2,000 dollars or more per user per year
Not suited for general sales teams or industries outside private capital and dealmaking
Automated email and calendar ingestion may raise data privacy considerations some firms need to evaluate
High annual minimum contract value makes it less accessible for very small or early-stage funds

faqs.question: What is Affinity used for?
faqs.answer: Affinity is a relationship intelligence CRM used by venture capital, private equity, and investment banking teams to manage deal sourcing, relationships, and portfolio tracking.

faqs.question: How is Affinity different from a normal CRM?
faqs.answer: Affinity automatically captures relationship data from email and calendar activity to build a network graph, rather than relying on manual contact logging like traditional CRMs.

faqs.question: How much does Affinity cost?
faqs.answer: Affinity does not publish self-serve pricing; industry references suggest roughly 2,000 dollars or more per user per year with an approximate 20,000 dollar annual minimum, quoted directly through sales.

faqs.question: Is Affinity the same as Affinity Photo or Affinity Designer?
faqs.answer: No, Affinity (affinity.co) is an unrelated relationship-intelligence CRM for dealmakers; Affinity Photo and Affinity Designer are separate creative software products made by Serif.

faqs.question: Who uses Affinity?
faqs.answer: Affinity is used primarily by venture capital firms, private equity firms, and investment banks, reportedly serving more than 3,000 customers across more than 70 countries.

faqs.question: When was Affinity founded?
faqs.answer: Affinity was founded in 2014 and is headquartered in San Francisco, California.

faqs.question: Does Affinity help with deal sourcing?
faqs.answer: Yes, Affinity's relationship scoring and warm-introduction discovery features are specifically designed to help dealmakers identify the best path to a new deal through existing team relationships.

faqs.question: Has Affinity raised venture funding?
faqs.answer: Yes, Affinity has raised institutional funding, including an 80 million dollar Series C round, from investors such as Menlo Ventures, Advance Venture Partners, 8VC, and MassMutual Ventures.

use_cases.title: Venture capital deal sourcing
use_cases.description: VC firms use Affinity to identify warm introduction paths to founders and manage their investment pipeline.

use_cases.title: Private equity relationship management
use_cases.description: Private equity firms use Affinity to track relationships with target companies, advisors, and co-investors across the deal lifecycle.

use_cases.title: Investment banking network intelligence
use_cases.description: Investment banks use Affinity's relationship graph to surface internal connections that can accelerate client and deal introductions.
use_cases.audience: Investment banking and capital markets teams

pricing_plans.price: Roughly 2,000 dollars per user per year, approximate 20,000 dollar annual minimum
features: Automated relationship capture and network graph
